speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape three, interview with survivor Nechama Shnoerson. It's November 16, 1995. Interviewer Charlotte Rettinger in Flushing, New York. So, shall we just continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we call the big Aktion. That's 9,200 people in one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said it broke the spirit--

speaker: interviewee
text: The impact on us as children already at this point was so bad. I mean, all we knew is every day it could happen to us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in one room. My whole family was-- we had just one room. And they tried to explain to us what this is all about. We have to stick together. And whatever will happen we will try to die together, so that none of us should be left alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is the only dream that we had. If we have to die, that we should die together. We should not be left without each other. And this is the kind of life we kept on living. Food was very, very little. There are many times that I, I took a piec-- a coat, or my mother told me to take this coat or take this pair of pants. I used to come over to the border and stand there and showing, and you know, because behind the border were Lithuanians that lived. And they used to come out and see it. That means I'm selling them.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'What do you want?' they used scream. Potatoes. So she used to come out, or he with a-- watch first of all that the German or the Lithuanian shouldn't be there close by. I used to throw over the coat or whatever I had. She used to throw over the border-- the gate-- potatoes. Grab it fast and bring it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made many exchanges that way with the Lithuanians. That was for food. But a lot of people had little kids. And they exchanged that for their children. They threw over the border many times a little baby, and so they could save them there. And I met later a little-- a boy, a man now, that this is the only way-- this is the way he was saved of this whole atrocity. But that's, that's not now to mention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life started to get kind of in a-- in a-- how will I say? An order? It wouldn't really be the right word to say, but-- Were you thinking routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a routine of men and women had to go outside and work. They were brigades. And every brigade had its own job to do. There were also many people that were taken from the ghetto and moved onto another country, which we found out later some of them wind up to be in Latvia. Some of them wind up to be in Estonia and some in Poland or some in Germany. But they used to bring also to our ghetto from other places for a while and then take them and kill them somewhere else, on, on, on a job. And you never saw them again coming back. These kind of movements was a, a daily life-- became a daily life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people were always coming and going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But many, many steady people-- like my father used to work there in a brigade and used to go out early in the morning and come back at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for a German-- for the Gestapo in the building where they li-- where they stayed, the Gestapo, as he was a butcher, if you recall. He was doing for them all the meats that they w-- that they need.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he land that job? Did he tell somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody knew of him. And that brigade was working only for that building-- for the cleaning and taking care of their, their rooms. So he wind up to be the in the kitchen. Sometimes he used to steal in some little pieces of meat or something, things that fell off. And he would ask a German if you can take it with him home. Sometimes they would let him do it. Or sometimes he used to just hide it inside his pants and bring it in because you are not allowed to do these kind of things. He would actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wind up being killed if they would have found it in him. But the hunger was so bad. He tried everything he could to bring in from the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do I understand it correctly that there was no food in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only rations. We were given bread--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, ration. Oh. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And we were bread and, and things that was the minimum of meat or this kind of thing. So it's all daily-- you had a right to come in to-- just no sugar or salt. Everything was on the grams, laid out to you, how much you can have a day or a week or a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So food was all the time very bad. We, we used to use potatoes when we used to get potatoes. We used to use even the peel of the potatoes. My mother found a way to use that for, for another kind of dish to make out a fit. Not to go anything in a waste. So this is the way we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sisters were working in the ghetto, where also places where the Germans took us to work. And many times, myself too, used to come to that place. We had to do certain things. It was a kind of a mill. They called it Werkstaetten. That it had different departments of different things to do. There was sewing. There were making pots and pans. There were making baskets. They were making hats-- all for the German army, of course. And the younger people used to go there. And they had to go there every day to work there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they accompanied there or did you go on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you had-- that now-- you could go-- you went there yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's because it was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was inside the ghetto, right. And I wound up to be there a couple of times too with my friends when they asked-- young people have to go, and so and so you have to go there. And you, you, you should be there at this and this time. And we had to go. And we did. And we went there. As a matter of fact, they even took us a couple of times to the airport. And we worked there, too, to clean up and do certain jobs. And they let us come back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other jobs besides cleaning? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto, in that Werkstaetten place, we did baskets made out of-- what they call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Reeds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reeds? Yes? You know those--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know, woven baskets?

speaker: interviewee
text: --natural-- yes, woven baskets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Straw, whatever, yeah, this is what we used to sit there on the floor and do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the airport, what work was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The airport was cleaning. Cleaning the airport, just shoveling away the unnecessary dirt that was accumulating.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: People, since it was already year-- a year and a year and a half passed by and people started to get used to the disastrous life. And believe it or not, you could find young people getting married in the ghetto. Women had children, after all it was still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any nursing care there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one hospital. And it was terrible. It was always over, over crowded, and no medicines. So if somebody needed help, it was hardly, hardly that you can get it. It was in the most prim-- primitive ways that if we went back to have children or if you needed some help in any illnesses. And people just died. They could have been helped, but they died because they didn't have the help. And that was called the hospital, but nothing really big help for that they could give and extend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any school functioning?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year we had a school. And it closed up right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans close it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They opened it, and then the closed it. We went this one year there. And not, not for the older ones. Only for the really young ones. Being the way we were, and young we were-- very young, as a matter of fact-- some evenings we used to have fun too, being that I had-- we had-- I had two older sisters. And they had friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So used to get together in our house in that little room that we lived, all of us together. And many evenings we used to sing and make ourselves happy the best way we could, and joke in our own lives, and cry together. And our home was still, again, all the time full of people and full of young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they loved my father and mother because they were so young and with always a smiling face and whatever they could share to help somebody else with a piece of bread or whatever she baked from those potato peels or peels from the carrots or whatever. And give out a sweater if anybody was cold or a pair of shoes. There was always somebody in our house together with us. I had a very dear girlfriend. We lived across the street from each other. And as she was to me just same as my sister. And she had a brother that was two years older than my sister-- older sister. And we brought those two families together. Her parents started to get very close to my parents. And her brother fell in love with my sister. And believe it or not, they got married while we were still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had another brother and another additional member in the family in the same little room. And this is how their wedding was in that little room. And that was their honeymoon in together with the parents and sisters. You can imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But always made a joke-- my father was a big joker-- always made a joke on every thing. He used to say, now get out. The young couple has to be there alone for a while. So we used to stay outside and wait. [LAUGHTER] This was-- but that was a normal. We were young, but we were so old at this point already. We knew everything that could be to make our lives easier and, and happier for a moment, if possible. We tried to help each other. I would say that the worst impact of life to me, personally, was the day that they came to take all the children away from the ghetto. That was an Aktion that destroyed actually me at this point, because I have seen how they did it and what they did to these kids. They came in with trucks and dogs-- big German shepherd dogs. And they went from house to house and screamed whoever has little kids to come outside. And they didn't take the mother and the child together. They just tore out the baby, or a child of four or five or 10 and threw them into those trucks. And the screaming and the crying and the begging, it was-- the skies were screaming together with the kids and the mothers and fathers. It was a disaster. They came to our house. My little sister was at this point only eight years old. We tried to hide her. So we-- so she was under the bed, hiding. She was laying there under the bed. And we said, not to say a word. Not-- we don't want to hear a-- nothing out of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans came in and saw there was children. Where are the children? You have any children? And we said, no, no, no, no. And he just turn his head down, and he said-- he saw my little sister there. This is not a child there? Come out here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came out. And she start crying and tearing his clothes off him. Please, don't take me. Please, don't take me. I will work. I'll do everything I can. Just don't take me away from my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was tearing him, his clothes and climbing on him. They gave her like a shove off from him. What the hell do you think you're doing here on top of me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The moment she fell off him, I grabbed her by hand, and we ran to the back door. And I didn't come back with her again. And she, she was saved at this moment. At this moment, she was saved. They walked out. It was a miracle from the second-- I don't know what the German felt at this moment. He got tired of her or whatever. And then he walked out. We saved her little sister. But what was going on in the streets, that's the worst, the worst that anybody could bear to watch it. What they did to the kids was very well known because, of course, they didn't bother with them. If a mother didn't tear-- give the baby away freely, so they let the dogs on that woman. And the dog would tear her apart until she would leave, of course, the baby off her hands. And then they used to take the baby and throw it in. Just actually throwing it into the trucks. Some of them took the babies by their legs and just beating them through the walls into the truck. It was-- it was-- it was just hard to describe what that was going on there. And we lost all our children. And really, that was-- took apart the people already to the-- to, to the grounds. You could never even talk to anybody anymore. It was-- everybody was torn apart in the most horrible way. It was in March 1944. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Politically at this point probably that was much older than I am because I was-- I was-- we understood that something is happening in the world [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through people that escaped, some of them, the ghetto. And they brought back news. Of course, there were partisans also that they ran away from the ghetto. And they somehow found a way to come back and, and bring in the news that the Russians are going to back again. And they're pushing the Germans out from Russia, and they're getting closer to Lithuania, because like Lithuania was in between Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- certain hopes started to get into people's minds. Maybe we'll somehow survive. And the Russians will get closer, and then the Russians will deliberate us. Maybe there could be a miracle like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But since the Germans had their way, and they had the last solution decided, whichever way, they'll get rid of all of them anyway. So they came in. And they-- it was in July. They started to clear out the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: July of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Many of the families, if there were families left, or singles, try to hide because actually that was so close to the Russians already coming to Lithuania that some of them that could escaped-- if they weren't caught in the roads and shot. But some of them escaped into the woods. The ones that were left in the ghetto tried to hide. What was the hiding place is to make a basement under your floor, in the basement, or make some ditches somehow to stay there by the time-- and wait until the Russians will come back. So my mother prepared some little things that we could have-- some food if in case we are going to be left there in the bottom-- in the basement. And we in our room did have a basement. But with so many people that came to ask us to be together with-- because they didn't have where to hide-- if they could stay with us. So everybody that asked us, we stayed. We were OK, OK. And we were laying there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- Ida, my middle sister, had trouble with her sinuses. And one evening she, she was almost choking to-- without air. There was no air. In the backyard was another little house with my-- with my mother's cousins living. So she-- we crawled-- they crawled out of the-- of our basement, asking our cousins if they wouldn't mind to keep my dau-- our-- my sister there, because they were not as packed there as in our room. And my sister was left there. The morning that she moved out from our basement to the other house, the Germans came in. And they started to clear out every house. For every-- from house to house, they went to see if somebody is left here because they're evacuating us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they screamed out, anybody in the house? Anybody in the house? Because if you're not going to get out of here, we will blow the house up, which they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, at this point, when they came into our house and they screamed, if you're not going to get out, if you're hiding, hiding somewhere, we will blow up the house. So my father, at this point, decided we were not going to be blown up or be burned to death. At least from a bullet, he said to all of us, it's going to be easier to die. Then let's get out of here. So we all moved out, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family. My family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did others stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without the sister. One sister was not with us. And all the friends and family that they stayed with us, we all got out of our bunker. And they told us we had to go because at this point they had already their minds made up to take us to the trains, to take us to Stutthof and Dachau. We didn't know. But, of course, we found out later. And they told us where to go, where we're going to stay there. From there, we're going to go somewhere else. While we were walking to that place, just getting out of our house-- of course, our house was bombed immediately and went on fire-- we could not even alert the people in the back-- that little house that where my sister was-- because with our house, the other one was burned too. Because they were very close to each other. And so my sister and the rest of the family, cousins wherever that were there, were burned to death. You could also see while we were walking in the street how people try to escape the flames from other homes that they bombed. And their bodies burning and, and running. And you couldn't even help them to take off-- running-- I mean, going in this sand to try to get off and throw over them some clothes or coats to take the fires off their bodies. It was a sight that I don't think any movie could do anything like it. My mother kept on holding me around. Don't look. Don't look. And you cannot-- you could not not see it because it was all around you. We got to that point where they collected all of us. And from there, they took us to the station where the trains were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the date is August--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They cleaned off, destroyed everything that was behind left, down to the grounds. The whole ghetto was cleaned off. We were taken to the wagons, to the trains. And we, all of us together, the men and women, pushed us into those cattle trains-- packed. There was no room even for-- you couldn't sit down, just standing right next to each other. And we were taken away, where nobody knew really where we're being taken. It was a night-- a day-- that night and a second day, we were traveling. And suddenly, the trains were stopped. What we did see though, every train had a tiny little-- every not train, every wagon had a tiny little window just on the top. And you'll believe it or not, some man tried to get through this little window to escape. Some women begged him to run away because there's no question about it. We knew that we were going to be sent to death now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of them try to escape that we pushed them through. But we heard also the bullets. Some of them were hit right away. And they were killed in the same place. Some of them escaped right through there, falling into the woods. And they escaped-- singles, not really much to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped-- they stopped us on one area. I don't know exactly where it was. And they asked everybody to get out. And at this point, they asked all the men to go on a side, and the women should go back to the trains. And this is when all the men were taken away from us, and my father among them. And the last look on his face, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] saying, we will meet again. And don't worry, I'll be OK. Just keep yourself alive, and we'll meet again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he able to say that or just thought that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, he said that. He said that. Sad, trying with bright eyes, but a sad face. And that was the last time I saw him. Where he was taken at this point, we didn't know. But we, we wind up in this concentration camp. It is called Stutthof. That's where they brought us in. What we had to do is get undressed, leave all our clothes right next to us, completely naked, go into a, a place where it was a shower. We were throwing over us water. Go out to another side. And there they gave us different clothing, whatever you could grab-- a pair of shoes, a skirt, a dress, whatever. But it was not ours. Whatever was left there, that wasn't ours. And they assigned on each one of us a number-- not tattooed, but just with a-- with a pencil, that at least we should know our number-- 5 4 2 9. And then they send us into barracks. And we stayed there that night. The second day, they asked us all of us to get out of the barracks and line up. And they start choosing. Some children were still with us-- children meaning not to any more younger than eight, nine, which my little sister was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every mother with a little girl like that was put on another line. Knowing that they was going to be taken away from me, I asked the German, can I come along? And he said, no. And I said, but I want to go together with my mother and my sister. And he said, no, you cannot. But I said, I do want to be with them. So he gave me with-- he had to stick in his hand. And he gave me over my head. I probably lost my conscious. But when I woke up from it, with a lot of my friends around me, they weren't there anymore. Let's pause now.
